original_image_tags: A line graph titled "LLV1" plotting "Breakup Percent (%)" on the vertical axis (0 to 100) against "Failure Time (sec)" on the horizontal axis (0 to 280). The graph contains three curves representing different values of "q-alpha in deg-lb/ft²": a solid line for q-alpha = 5,000, a dashed line for q-alpha = 10,000, and a dotted line for q-alpha = 20,000. All curves show a near 100% breakup rate initially, followed by a sharp decline. The decline for q-alpha = 20,000 starts earliest (around 60 seconds), followed by q-alpha = 10,000 (around 70 seconds), and then q-alpha = 5,000 (around 80 seconds). All curves reach approximately 30% breakup by 100 seconds, then gradually decline to 0% by approximately 220 seconds.

elements.description: A line graph titled 'Figure 29. LLV1 Breakup Percentages' plotting 'Breakup Percent (%)' on the y-axis (0 to 100) against 'Failure Time (sec)' on the x-axis (0 to 280). It displays three data series representing different 'q-alpha in deg-lb/ft²' values: 5,000 (solid line), 10,000 (dashed line), and 20,000 (dotted line). The graph is labeled 'LLV1' and shows that the percentages drop from near 100% to lower values as failure time increases.

judge.reason: Both descriptions agree on all factual visual content: a line graph with the same title, axes, scales, three curves with identical q-alpha values and line styles, and the same overall trend of breakup percentages declining over failure time.
